LEGENDARY

GAME

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 135

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 136

Legendary Horizons

 
 
T

he game master is the great director of any
TTRPG. The following sections assume you
are the one building the world, running the
foes, and managing the table–not playing in
it.

You might have heard that high-level play
breaks things that worked fine at lower
levels. This is true. Saving throws become trivial or
impossible. Single spells can remove a foe from the fight
entirely. One player’s toolkit can invalidate another’s. Do
not allow these difficulties to intimidate you; running a
Legendary Campaign is a unique, fulfilling act that will
take you and your group on an unforgettable adventure.

The systems present in this sourcebook exist to address
those problems directly, but they require mastery.
Reading this section before running your first Legendary
session will save you significant trouble at the table.

Do not expect perfection, either from you or from your
players, and communicate clearly if something isn’t
working as intended. They are your collaborators in this
Legendary Game, not your opponents–at this tier of play,
the level of power you wield can believably wipe them out
with a single godly wish, no matter how powerful their
abilities.

The Three Pillars. We’ve touched upon the three pillars
of D&D in the introduction of this book. The following
chapter is a more detailed analysis of how these
fundamental aspects change from the DM’s side.
Exploration

First is exploration, in which we’ll discuss worldbuilding,
creating incredible locations, and developing obstacles

THE LEGENDARY GAME

your characters may encounter while not actively in a
combat or roleplay scenario.
Roleplay

Second is roleplay, focused on narrative tension at
legendary levels, epic NPCs, and conversation-type
encounters. This section will help you create and balance
the story in a way that still affects your players, even as
they ascend to godhood.
Combat

Third is combat. This section will teach you the general
principles of high-level combat, how to make it quick and
fun, and how to manage your party’s combat resources
over the course of a Legendary Adventure. It will include
several ways of using non-Legendary foes to bring
difficulty to your players, along with many different types
of combat encounters to suit your Legendary needs.
To Battle Legends

The final section of this chapter will focus exclusively on
Legendary Battles, the kind that this entire sourcebook
builds up to. It will give you the tools to understand what
makes a legendary showdown fun, the phases such an
encounter might go through, how to maintain tension
over several hours of combat, how to create compelling
environments and lairs, and some tips and tricks to make
sure you are fully prepared for your final battle.

This section, in particular, is perfect to construct the
final battle of a campaign, be it legendary or not. Most of
the same precepts presented apply to both a 25th-level
boss fight and a 15th-level one.

Original PDF page 137

Exploration

Delving into the deepest dungeons, uncovering the
secrets of ancient civilizations, charting planes no mortal
has named–these are the adventures your Legendary
players will routinely find themselves embarking on. To
run a truly memorable high-level campaign, the locations
must meet the scope of the party standing in them.

Epic Worldbuilding

The first thing you must do when preparing a Legendary
Game is to settle on your narrative. What kind of story will
you tell beyond 20th level? Will your players chase down a
newborn God with plans to conquer the world? Battle
across an intergalactic war, traveling through planets and
dismantling an empire that ruled the stars for a thousand
years? Hunt the artifacts of a fallen Legendary Hero in the
hopes of ascending to Godhood themselves?
Understanding your Legendary narrative, separate from
whatever brought the party to 20th level, will shape the
settings your campaign demands. “Settings”, plural; a
Legendary Game visits many locations, and grapples with
cultures, worlds, and planes of existence that were only
hinted at before.

Perhaps your party has already passed through the
hells or heavens on some earlier adventure–a quick item
retrieval, a petition for aid, a rescue of an NPC. Now is the
moment to develop those glimpsed places into fullyfledged worlds of magic and might. The planes beyond
the Material are not merely exotic backdrops; they are

powerful environments where the median creature far
exceeds the typical mortal in strength and danger. A
Legendary Party still stands beyond most of what they
will encounter, but beings such as Solars, Pit Fiends,
Liches, and Dragons may populate these places by the
dozens. And beyond them, stranger things still, creatures
that don’t even have a stat block for your party to fight.
Titans of stone with thousands of hit points, entities with
dominion over the laws of reality, eldritch monstrosities
capable of devouring entire planets…
The Planescape

A coherent cosmology is crucial for a Legendary Game,
even if your story unfolds primarily within a single
dimension. Your party almost certainly has access to
plane-hopping spells or artifacts by this point, and they
will likely use them to chase any thread you dangle in
front of them. The Outer Planes of the Forgotten Realms
setting offer a well-developed example, built over decades,
and are worth studying as a structural model for your own
cosmic wheel. Our own setting, the Universe of Strings,
draws heavily from that tradition and can serve as a
complete foundation for a Legendary campaign if you
need one.

That said, we recommend using other settings as
inspiration rather than adoption. The worldbuilding
demands of a Legendary Game tend to outgrow any
single published source, and running a pre-existing
planescape carries real risk–your players may be a Google
search away from spoilers. At this tier, you alone must be
the master of the stakes.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 138

The Fundamental Forces

A cosmology needs opposing tensions to feel alive.
Rather than borrowing alignment axes wholesale from
5e, consider building your cosmos around forces that
reflect your setting’s history. These are some starting
points, along with key questions you should know the
answer to when creating a Legendary Campaign.

Order and Chaos. Perhaps the oldest opposition.
Where Order solidifies, civilizations form, laws are set
in place, and reality becomes predictable. Where Chaos
reigns, possibility proliferates, but shapes remain
fleeting and ever-changing. The material plane is a
balance of these two forces; when creating worlds
beyond, think about tilting the balance to one side or the
other. On the far end of Order, you can find infinite
cities, totalitarian civilizations, perfectly ordered stars of
suffocating law. Upon the realms of chaos, you can find
impossible landscapes, beautiful and untamed wilds,
uninhabitable planes of madness and chaos ruled by
fleeting might and uncontrollable magic.

Creation and Void. The vastness of space can be as
big or as small as you choose. What are the distances
between your worlds? Where do they draw their planar
borders? Are they infinite dimensions contained within
the greater infinity of the cosmos? Or do they carve their
small domains upon the overwhelming void between
stars? Think about the scales of your universe. Where
does it end? How do planewalkers travel? Is everything
done with magic? How is that magic regulated upon
different planes of existence? These questions are all
connected to the dichotomy between Creation (where
something exists) and Void (where nothing exists).

Life and Death. Perhaps as important–if not
more so–than the previous forces, Life and
Death define the structure of your cosmology. What
happens after death? Where does the soul go? In
traditional 5e fashion, the planes of existence often
function as afterlives—but how does the life of a devil
compare to that of a mortal? Are the people of your
world judged for their brief lives and confined to eternal
fates? In a cosmos where mortals may interact with
elder beings of unknowable power, devils who bargain
for souls, and angels who guard the resting places of
heroes, what does death truly mean?

What happens when an angel is destroyed? What of a
devil or an elemental? Do they possess an essence that
remains, or do they fade into nothingness? Perhaps they
are immortal, endlessly reformed within their native
planes. If so, how do they reproduce? How do they
manage populations that might grow without limit?

Do all heroes and villains become something else
after death? Do tyrants rise as devils, and heroes as
angels? Do they continue their conflicts as eternal
beings, or forget their mortal lives entirely in service to a
greater purpose?

Each question you ask helps define the cycle of Life
and Death in your universe, until your Legendary party
can interact with these forces directly.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Many More! Worldbuilding is, at its core, an exercise in
asking questions. To support a Legendary game, your
setting must be robust, and the questions you asked
before 20th level may no longer suffice at this new scale
of power. New ones will take their place; not simply
“Who are the gods?” but “How did they become gods?”

If your players uncover something that isn’t fully
developed, take a moment to consider before
answering–or perhaps leave it unanswered until you can
weave it into your broader worldbuilding. Stay
mysterious! A Legendary Campaign will often push your
worldbuilding engines to their absolute limit.

Original PDF page 139

Legendary Locations

From epic dungeons to inconceivable landscapes, a
Legendary Campaign will take your party through some
of the most impressive locations in the cosmos. Alien
worlds, forgotten kingdoms, dangerous asteroid belts;
each will determine the scope of the session they frame.
The environments should be as vibrant and complex, if
not more so, than the combat or roleplay scenarios.
What makes a good location?

There are several principles to follow when
conceptualizing the environment your Legendary party
will inhabit. Coincidentally, they all start with the letter
“I”:

Impressiveness. A Legendary game demands
legendary locations. Make your environments
otherworldly, incredible, and awe-inspiring. You are no
longer depicting simple towns, rolling hills, or ruined
dungeons. Instead, imagine cities built on the dark side
of tidally locked planets, where their inhabitants find
refuge from the scorching heat of a dying star. Think of
floating mountains connected by strands of condensed
clouds, ever-shifting and yet, for fleeting moments,
capable of supporting the weight of material beings.
Describe the astral bastion of a long-forgotten lich,
teeming with the confined magic of a thousand years,
and the blasphemous creatures once created to serve it.

One of the greatest joys of running a Legendary
campaign is creating locations that simply cannot exist
in a more grounded game. Asteroid fields where an
ancient, planet-sized being cultivates its crops; eternal

cities at the heart of the Hells, where archdevils wrestle
for power amid the suffering of billions; living
constellations that once housed a galaxy-spanning
empire of highly advanced beings, now fallen into ruin.

Go all out! Make sure your players remember the
places they visit.

Importance. A Legendary location must matter. Your
environments should carry weight within the story, the
cosmos, or the characters themselves. Ask yourself: why
does this place exist, and why does it matter now? A
floating city is impressive, but it becomes memorable
when it is the last refuge of a dying civilization, or the
site where a grand lich is conducting a deathly ritual.

Tie your locations to consequences. The astral tomb
is not merely a relic of the past–it may hold a sealed
Thread of Divinity, one whose release could reshape
entire planes. The palace at the heart of the crucible is
not just the abode of an archdevil–it might be the place
where a contract bearing a character’s soul is kept.
When players understand that their actions within a
location have lasting effects, the environment
transforms from a backdrop into a driving force of the
narrative.

Importance also anchors player motivation. A place
tied to a character’s past, a looming cosmic threat, or an
important answer will naturally draw the party’s
attention and investment. In Legendary play, where the
scale of the universe expands dramatically, grounding
your locations in meaning ensures that your players do

not feel lost in the vastness of it all and in their

 many possible  destinations.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

text.content:
Original PDF page 140

Interactivity. What is an epic location if there is only
one path forward, one thing to do, one objective to
fulfill? As impressive as an environment might be, it is
equally important for you to create multiple avenues of
interactivity within it, allowing for player creativity. Most
of the time, this simply means being open to your party’s
choices; perhaps they fly over the ruined city instead of
exploring it in detail. Perhaps they focus on a damned
soul you described in passing while traversing the Hells,
or find a way to breach the ancient bastion’s doors
without ever needing the key. Interactivity is, more often
than not, a result of your capacity for improvisation–not
your ability to prepare everything in advance. Leave
threads open in each location, and see which ones your
party chooses to follow. Being a DM is guiding the party
toward the right goal, even when they take a completely
different path than the one you intended.

In Legendary Locations, especially, your players
possess such an array of abilities and options that you
often cannot predict the course of action they might
take. You need to learn to roll with the punches and
keep yourself flexible enough to relocate certain NPCs,
puzzles, or combat encounters to fit the will of your
players.
Event Encounters

There is one type of encounter that is very much linked
to Legendary Locations: the Event-type encounter.
These moments in the game are cinematic in nature,
descriptive, and require little to no input from the party.
A dragon flying in the distance, a circle of fey lights
congregating around a tree, an execution of criminals
carried out in the name of the King–these narrative
moments serve to deepen the world and spotlight a
certain aspect of the story.

In legendary campaigns, these event-type encounters
take on a different role, since the chance of your players
interacting with them becomes a lot higher. Perhaps the
Legendary Ranger chooses to shoot down the dragon
you described several miles off. Perhaps the druid
commands the fey lights to aid the party. Perhaps the
paladin, with greater authority than the king, chooses to
spare the criminals–or takes up the blade and executes
them. Be ready to improvise!

When showcasing narrative events, it helps to be
grander than you were in your non-Legendary game. As
the party travels through the astral sea, they witness the
birth of a new star, or the rending of a planet by a
meteor shower. While the characters are exploring the
elemental plane of earth, giants of stone battle in the
distance, breaking and reforming in a game of titanic
proportions. Amid the heroes’ infiltration through the
castle of a demon lord, they pass through a great door
powered by an enormous machine of steel and flesh,
where thousands of damned souls cry in pain with each
of the contraptions’ blasphemous movements.

Always remember, the party might choose to interact
with these events! Be ready to allow such tampering,
and think on your feet to get the party back on track.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

DM: The lands of the fae stretch out before you, a forest of
golden leaves parted through the middle by a set of
enormous blade-like karsts. Far in the sky float several
islands, orbiting above the forest as if something at the
center of the myriad of golden trees kept them from falling.
A river of honey flows through one of the far-off islands,
oozing down toward the ground and curving as it falls…

Celes, playing Agamena the Barbarian: Oh my god, did you
say river of honey? That’s absolutely insane! Let’s go there!

DM: Uh, it’s floating very far from the ground, about two or
three miles off…

Mari, playing Kyran the Sorcerer. Do not worry, Agamena. I’ll
teleport us there.

DM, who just improvised the place to add some flavour: With
a space-bending word, you teleport the party to the honeyriver island. As you manifest, you feel yourselves float, light
as feathers. Something’s affecting the gravity of everything
above the golden forest. Perhaps it has something to do
with the sage you’ve been looking for in–

Celes. Can I drink from the river? Is it good? Is it like, nectar
or something?

DM, thinking of a way to get the party back on track: …Alright.
You drop to your knees and gather a bit of honey on your
palm before tasting it. The flavour is unreal, perhaps the
best sustenance you’ve ever had. It must be something
divine, for it doesn’t even stain your fingers or fill your belly.
You gain 10 temporary hit points.

Celes: Let’s go! I’m over 400 hit points at last!

DM: Before you finish licking your lips, however, you feel a
foot push your head into the honey. Make a Strength Saving
throw.

Celes: Oh damn! Alright, low roll, that’s 28, but Indomitable
Might makes it so I cannot roll beneath my strength score.
That’s 30, DM.

DM, changing the place of an important NPC: Your muscles
tighten to fight the pushing force, but despite your heroic
might, you fail. Gravity itself betrays you, as you sink into
the honeyed flow. A figure appears from thin air above
Agamena, his elfine boot pushing her head down into the
water. “Who darrres violate my rrriver?” the powerful voice
says. His power over gravity seems absolute. This must be
him–the Sage of the Golden Forest. Kyran, your sorcerous
prowess allows you to sense the warping gravity connecting
in threads to his golden, leaf-adorned crown.

Mari. Oh shoot… He beat Agamena’s 30?

DM. Yes. The sage seems to have power over much heavier
things than your friend.

Original PDF page 141

Obstacles

Finally, when depicting the environments, worlds, and
locations your Legendary Party will explore, it helps to
create believable obstacles that can challenge
characters beyond 20th level. In non-Legendary
campaigns, these might take the shape of skill
challenges, such as stealthing through a dungeon,
climbing a mountain, finding a path through the
wilderness; they might also be decision-making tests,
planning how to assault a bandit camp, choosing which
path to take at the crossroads, or even what lead to
follow when solving a mystery.

A Legendary Campaign requires a different
perspective on obstacles–with higher ability scores, a
plethora of options, and much greater scope.

Skill Checks

To create satisfying skill checks, first acknowledge your
party’s mastery of non-Legendary challenges by not
inflating DCs without reason. Just because the rogue is
rolling a 42 on acrobatics does not mean the DC for a
backflip should increase from 15 to 30. Success should
feel earned and rewarding–let your players experience
how far they’ve come by overcoming once-difficult tasks
with ease.

Now, what constitutes a DC 30, 35, 40, or even 50
skill check in Exploration? Three aspects: complexity,
impressiveness, and narrative importance.

Complexity. The first is simple to determine–how hard
is this challenge? Perhaps a backflip has a DC of 15.
The rogue, however, is trying to do one in combat by
jumping on top of the barbarian (DC 18) and changing
direction mid air to grab unto the ceiling (DC 23) only to
then disarm an explosive trap armed into the roof (DC
27) in the few seconds before it goes off (DC 33), all in
one bonus action (DC 40). With that aforementioned 42,
the Rogue clears it. Don’t gatekeep success, even when
it stretches plausibility; you are playing a Legendary
Campaign, the characters should be able to do
Legendary stuff!

Some challenges might be harder to determine. A
good rule to follow is this: if you intend them to be
complex, put a high DC on them. Perhaps a lockpicking
challenge hides a key artifact. The lockbox you
described, though magical, doesn’t have nearly the
same importance as the secret gates to a penumbral
fiend’s laboratory; yet both can have a DC of 35 or even
40. A specialized character will clear both without much
trouble.

Impressiveness. The DCs beyond 30 are reserved for
acts that non-Legendary characters cannot ever hope to
accomplish. A DC 35 Athletics check might be required
to hold up the side of a falling building. A DC 40
Survival check may be appropriate to follow the residual
arcane trace left behind by an ethereal creature. A DC
45 History check might reconstruct an ancient prophecy
from scribbled runes on the side of fragmented ruins.
The more extraordinary the feat, the higher the DC.

Narrative Importance. Some skill checks are
required to further the story. These should often be
challenging, even for a Legendary Party. Depending on
the average range of your character’s rolls, you may
choose to increase a DC if you believe it to be of
sufficient weight within the story to pose a greater
challenge. If your Legendary Barbarian is trying to pull
a mighty sword of ancient legend from a stone at the
heart of the dungeon, and they happen to be inspired by
a Bard and enhanced by a Cleric, you can very well put a
DC of 50 on that athletics check, even if such a DC
could be representative of a much more epic feat of
strength in another circumstance.
Dilemmas

While decisive moments most often arise in roleplaying
scenarios, they can just as easily emerge during
exploration or even in the midst of combat. These are
moments that do not hinge on a die roll, but instead
determine the path the party chooses to follow, each
with its own pros and cons.

Exploration dilemmas for a Legendary party function
much like those in non-legendary play–the difference
lies in their scale, stakes, and consequence. Rather than
choosing which road to take at a crossroads, the party
might decide which plane of existence to explore.
Instead of determining how to approach a gnoll camp,
they may plan the infiltration of the seat of a Goddess of
Darkness. In place of debating whether to take a magic
item from an ancient ruin or leave it be, they might
weigh the consequences of claiming a shard of divinity–
or leaving it behind.

To maintain tension in these decision-driven
encounters, ensure that your players understand the
stakes. A meaningful choice is rarely an easy one!
Traps & Puzzles

When it comes to exploration, traps and puzzles are the
most iconic encounters your characters might face.

Traps are easy to scale up to Legendary levels – more
powerful spells stored in glyphs of warding, deadlier
poisons, and, if you are feeling creative, grander and
more complex mechanisms. A never-ending hallway, a
perpetual ray of disintegration, a room of lethal,
unrelenting heat. In our experience, Legendary traps
should deal damage proportional to the tier of play–
roughly 50 points per trigger at Legend, 75 at Paragon,
and 100 or more at Ascended, depending on their
intended deadliness.

Puzzles can remain mostly the same as their nonLegendary counterparts, only with greater narrative
weight. Solving the ancient riddle of a sphinx, finding
the path through an infinite labyrinth, and discovering
the missing piece of a heavenly sculpture–these are all
puzzles that can exist in both regular and Legendary
campaigns. Keep in mind the increased capabilities of
Legendary Characters and consider requiring their
powerful spells or mythical techniques to solve certain
puzzles. To balance different magical effects, see the
Magic section in General Rules.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 142

Roleplay

Your Legendary Party will interact with the greatest
creatures in your setting, be it Gods, devils, or eldritch
horrors beyond imagining. Not only that, but these
interactions will often be on equal ground–a cast of
legendary heroes can very well pose a threat to even
these mighty beings. As the DM, you must incarnate
these incredible forces of existence and know how to
create narrative tension in a cosmos of ever-rising
stakes.

The Wills at Play

In the Epic Worldbuilding section, you established the
broad architecture of your cosmos–the planescape, the
dominant powers, the Gods that preside over it all. That
is your backdrop. This section is about the foreground:
the individual wills, ambitions, and conflicts that
actually move the story from one session to the next.

Start by narrowing your scope to the personal level.
Who wants something badly enough to act on it? This is
the question that begins an epic narrative. In a setting
where the ultimate power is not only well defined but
very much in reach for the party, those who act upon
your story should come to challenge the fundamental
order you’ve established when building your universe.
A Living World

Build a world that was already in motion before the
party arrived. Other heroes exist. Other factions are
pursuing their own solutions to the same problems. A
conclave of Ascended beings attempting a diplomatic
resolution to a greater conflict. A guerrilla movement of
enslaved creatures whose methods are questionable but
whose cause is just. A legendary figure from an earlier
age who has been fighting alone for centuries and has
strong opinions about the newcomers.

These groups give the party a world to engage
with rather than a vacuum to fill, and they create
natural dramatic pressure–because if the party
moves too slowly, someone else will act, and
the shape of the story will change accordingly.
Hierarchies

A God of Order may preside over an entire dimension, a
tyrannical creature unbothered by the changing tides of
the universe, but it is their high priest–ambitious,
brilliant, and quietly convinced that their deity’s
methods are insufficient–who gives the party the task of
tearing the God down.

Lesser powers are essential in any campaign. In a
Legendary Game, these characters can become the
driving force of the narrative, even while being lesser
than the party. Build hierarchies; complex, difficult to
judge, with many twists and turns. Beneath your
primary divine forces, place figures of significant power,
more keen on stirring the pot: demigods licking old
wounds, wielders of great artifacts, warlords with
enough planar territory to ignore their supposed
masters. These actors do not need to be fully detailed

THE LEGENDARY GAME

from the start—they only need to want things, and those
wants should pull in different directions. A lesser God
who quietly aids both sides of a celestial war, a fallen
angel who believes the current divine order is corrupt, a
devil who has been gaining power over millennia. These
are the wills that, much like your party, come to
threaten the status quo of your universe.
The Party

The most important thing to protect, through all of this
complexity, is the party’s centrality. It is easy, when
building a cosmos full of ancient and powerful beings, to
write a story in which those beings do mostly
everything. This is not very engaging for your players.

The Gods and factions and lesser powers should be
forces that the party navigates, influencing allies, slaying
enemies, and determining the fate of worlds–never
spectacles they watch from a distance. When the
conclave of Ascended beings convenes to decide the
future of a dying plane, the party should be in the room.
When the second-in-command finally moves against
their master, it should be because the party gave them
the reason, the means, or the moment. When the God of
Darkness finally reveals itself, it should be the party he
fights against.

Your players should always be at the center of the
story, tipping the scales of the cosmos and determining
the fate of your setting.

Original PDF page 143

Epic NPCs

NPCs are the key to creating a meaningful Legendary
Campaign. The party needs people to fight,
personalities to encounter, and figures compelling
enough to make the cosmos feel like it is populated by
more than just obstacles and exposition. At this tier of
play, the gloves are off–you can create incredibly
powerful heroes and villains, yet you can also make
regular people, trying to survive in a world of epic
proportions. The NPCs your players will remember do
not have to be the ones with the most detailed stat
blocks, but instead those that have the clearest wants,
the most interesting contradictions, and the most
personal stakes in what the party chooses to do.

What makes an epic NPC? Quite simply, a character
that, in some way or another, interacts with the
Legendary levels and/or plot of your campaign. This
includes not only your elder wizard NPC, who has
guided the party across many arcs, but also the
mysterious son of a blacksmith who has been marked
by an evil God the party is trying to destroy. Though
most Epic NPCs are mainly defined by their power,
some characters can still be Epic while being much
weaker than the party; what makes them “epic”, in this
case, is not their capabilities, but rather their narrative
importance.
Building an Epic NPC

When creating an Epic NPC, treat your process much
like you would a non-Legendary addition to your game.

Establish first what the NPC wants and what they are
willing to do to get it. A Solar who wants to restore their
fallen God to glory and is willing to sacrifice a mortal
civilization to do so is immediately more interesting
than a Solar who’s antagonistic because their God
happens to be evil. Want creates behavior, and behavior
is what the party will actually encounter at the table.
Everything else–the stat block, the backstory, the
interactions–exists to explain and complicate that
central want.

Afterwards, give your Epic NPCs a history worthy of
the title “Epic”. Some creatures you will incarnate have
been alive since before the party began adventuring.
They have won and lost, and made compromises they
have not fully reckoned with. A Threadbearer who
seized their divine shard in a moment of desperation
centuries ago and has spent every year since regretting
it, a devil who was a great paladin in life and gave
themselves to the hells in one final act of sacrifice, a
prince with only good intentions forced to do their
tyrannical father’s bidding.

This backstory does not need to be delivered as
exposition, but rather needs to be visible in how they
behave–in what they flinch at, what they cannot quite
bring themselves to say. These nuances are what the
party can interact with in roleplay scenarios, influencing
the NPCs without the need for a fight.

In addition, remember to create a clear relationship
between the NPC and the party, something to anchor

you to their specific character when it comes to
roleplaying them. Not simply ally or enemy, but
something with depth; a demigod who owes the party a
debt yet feels humiliated by it, a king who requests
errands from the characters only to ensure they are not
a threat to his rule, a villain’s second-in-command who
has begun to wonder if they are backing the wrong side.
These relationships give your NPCs a reason to be in
certain situations, and give the party a reason to care
about them beyond their immediate utility.
Roleplaying Epic

The most common mistake DMs make with powerful
NPCs is making them feel omniscient. Ancient beings
know a great deal, but not all should feel like they read
minds or know everything there is to know about the
party. Let the NPC be wrong about something
important, and let the party either correct them or use
their mistake as an advantage.

Powerful NPCs should also have manners
appropriate to their station, which creates natural
friction with a party accustomed to being the most
significant force in any room. An archdevil does not
respect mortals, even Legendary ones–not out of malice,
but out of a deeply ingrained assumption that they are
superior. A God does not ask; they require–demand,
even–and expect their will to be done. Playing these
social textures consistently makes encounters with Epic
NPCs feel like genuine contact with something that
exists beyond the party, something that has long ago
gained its power and isn’t about to respect any
newcomer.

What changes that dynamic, and what should drive
some of your most memorable NPC scenes, is the
moment these beings are forced to reconsider their
assumptions about the party. The archdevil may have to
ask for help. The God who does not have the answer to
a question. Cracking a prideful creature’s haughty
demeanor is one of the most satisfying things for the
players to accomplish. They are newcomers to the
cosmos, yes–but they will be respected.

“I’ve walked this world since before your
civilization had a name. In that time, I
have met perhaps a handful of mortals
worth the trouble of an audience. You
come to my monastery in shambles, yet
your souls–your flame–is greater than
that of my most accomplished pupils. Sit.
Speak. You’ve earned the privilege… of
my attention.”

– Yrith Yuna, the Sapphire Dragon

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 144

Power and the Party

Perhaps the trickiest things to manage in a Legendary
Campaign are the power differential between the party
and the NPCs around them. Get it wrong in one
direction, and the party feels irrelevant, dwarfed by
beings so far beyond them that their choices do not
matter. Get it wrong in the other, and the cosmos feels
hollow, populated by figures who are supposedly
ancient and terrible but fold very quickly under the
party’s might.

Create NPCs with circumstantial power, influence
that cannot be quantified by a stat block or ability. An
influential demigod might not be a threat to a Legendary
Barbarian, but they do control most of the alliances in
the cosmos, and the party needs those connections. A
simple cultist can be as dangerous as their God if their
death releases a great curse upon the land. A
planeswalking trader of information may withhold a
secret from the party until they complete an errand.
Hard Power

If you need your NPC to be more of a physical threat to
the party, on the other hand, you can mix and match the
features available to your players in the previous
chapters to create a challenging creature. Not only will
you be showcasing the full extent of this book’s
mechanical options, but you will also be challenging
your players with their own tools, perhaps showing
them ways to use them differently. Browse through the
Heroic Path features, Mythical Techniques, and 10thlevel spells like a catalogue, and take features from
different classes to create your own powerful NPC.
Make sure to use this book to its full potential by
expanding on the classes or aspects not explored by the
players.

Another way to make your NPCs threatening in
combat is to give them a specific, meaningful advantage
against the party–immunity to a type of damage the
characters rely on heavily, an ability that punishes
certain spells and effects, an action that is especially
effective against your players. Remember, D&D is a
game about information. A creature can pose much
more of a challenge in your hands by simply exploiting
your party’s weak points, without it having to be an
untouchable god that cannot be harmed.

You can also balance an NPC by giving them a
singular overpowered ability that the party must respect
or find a way to overcome. Power over gravity; anyone
who attempts to move in the NPC’s area without their
permission must succeed on a DC 30 Strength saving
throw or be immobilized. Power over the future; the
NPC can change any roll that they can see to a number
of their choosing. Power over death; any mortal who
touches the NPC must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution
saving throw or die. These incredibly powerful abilities
are a great way to give a character that extra
otherworldly power that feels beyond even a Legendary
Party, yet can be overcome regardless through clever
planning, raw muscle, or Legendary Resistances.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Finally, you can use the stat blocks of our Legendary
Foes in Chapter VI to ensure the NPCs can pose a real
threat to the party. Perhaps the Guardians of Aaru are
the perfect template for your divine adjudicator and
enforcer. Perhaps Barbatos, Wielder of Triumph, is
exactly what you need to ensure a grand warlord is
threatening. Perhaps the Moonsilver Jester has the
abilities your mysterious mage needs to keep up with
the party. Use this book to its full potential!

Barring certain Gods and great figures of authority in
your cosmos, Epic NPCs should never feel impossible
to beat in a straight-up fight. Your party is Legendary;
treat their potential as such. Be smarter about the
challenge you create.

When you do require beings much greater than your
characters, however…
Creatures Beyond

Even in a Legendary Campaign, some NPCs should feel
beyond the party’s combat capabilities. Gods, eldritch
monstrosities, fundamental beings of reality–give these
creatures power that cannot be overcome without great
preparation. Some of the Ascended-tier foes on Chapter
VI can be a great template for these types of beings,
although you might have to crank some of their features
a bit–those stat blocks are meant to be beaten, after all,
and you might need creatures in your narrative that
should not even be capable of bending.

Even in the most extreme cases, however, always
respect the players. Legendary Points allow them to
resist a certain number of effects–make sure they at
least have the chance to do so. Non-legendary
characters cannot even hope to withstand a God’s will;
Legendary Characters can at least try.

“You have meddled with powers beyond
your understanding, and yet here you
stand. Impressive. But power borrowed is
not power owned, and the favor of lesser
beings means nothing before true
divinity. Kneel, and I’ll let you live.
Raise your hand against me, and the
might of Tartarus entire will descend
upon your meager plane and cull your
kind to the last. Your arrogance will be
my excuse. I will wipe your pathetic race
from the face of creation. Unless. You.
Kneel.“

– Barbatos, Wielder of Triumph

Original PDF page 145

Roleplay Encounters

Not every confrontation in a Legendary Campaign ends
with someone at zero hit points. At this tier, the party
moves through courts of ancient powers, negotiates
with beings that have outlasted civilizations, and deals
with alliances that shift with the cosmic tides of war. A
well-constructed roleplay encounter can carry as much
weight as any combat or exploration event–indeed,
some conversations can reshape the narrative entirely.

The following are some encounter templates that may
be useful for your Legendary Game.

Audience. The party has secured a meeting with
someone of great importance; a God, an ancient power,
or a sage that rarely receives visitors. The pressure of
an Audience comes from asymmetry–the NPC holds
most of the leverage, and the party must make their
case without the option of force as a fallback (usually).
These encounters reward preparation, creativity, and
quick thinking. The party that arrives knowing what the
NPC wants, what they fear, and what they consider
beneath their attention will navigate an Audience very
differently from the party that walks in expecting to be
impressive enough on their own merits. In addition, the
information revealed within the Audience encounter
often changes the paradigm, and your party will have to
adapt to it if they hope to succeed. Run these
encounters with ceremony, ritual, and the mystique
appropriate for the situation you created. Should the
party not live up to the NPC’s expectations, have them
dismissed.

Confrontation. The party and an NPC are in direct,
personal conflict–but the situation either hasn’t yet
escalated to violence, or makes combat impossible or
inadvisable. A confrontation with a villain on neutral
ground where both parties have agreed to parley. A
negotiation between warring factions gone south. A
conversation between opposing characters who are too
wounded to continue fighting. These encounters are
about what is said when the mask comes off, and they
require you, as the DM, to know the NPC’s most
emotional truths and improvise under pressure.
Confrontations often reveal things that couldn’t be said
in other settings, creating some of the most important
moments in a campaign’s narrative.

Dilemma. The party is forced to choose between two
(or more) mutually exclusive outcomes, each bearing
meaningful consequences. These decisions often
determine the fate of important NPCs, entire factions,
or even planes of existence. Each option should be
clearly embodied–typically by an NPC, group, or cause
that stands to gain (or suffer) from the party’s choice.
Dilemmas are one of the clearest expressions of a
Legendary Party’s influence on the world. At these
levels, the characters’ choices determine the fate of
nations. A well-crafted Dilemma ensures that every path
involves sacrifice, risk, or unintended consequences.
The party might be forced to choose between saving a

celestial from certain death or pursuing a fleeing devil
whose escape would doom another world–no decision
is perfect, and each determines the outcome of great
and powerful creatures.

Information. Some encounters are created to ensure
the party gains a certain piece of information. Events
where two NPCs interact, conversations without
particular stakes that help to inform the narrative, or
even encounters during travel that flesh out the world.
In Legendary Campaigns, these encounters should feel
impressive, grand, legendary. Two Gods agreeing to
split a plane of existence while the party watches, a
conversation with an ancient hero about their long-lost
legendary artifact, an epic introduction of an important
NPC.

Negotiation. Both parties want something the other
has, and neither is willing to simply give it. Unlike the
Audience, a Negotiation implies somewhat equal
grounds–the party has leverage, and the NPC knows it.
These encounters are best run with clear stakes
established before the conversation begins. What does
each side want? What is each side willing to concede?
What are the dealbreakers? A Negotiation that drifts
without those anchors becomes a boring conversation
that seems to go nowhere. Make sure the stakes are
well defined, and don’t be afraid to escalate. A
Negotiation in a Legendary Game is marked by the
pride and power of the parties at play. The NPCs will
not be disrespected, nor will they waste their time with
offers beneath their station. Negotiations can become
Confrontations, and even turn into outright combat,
should the party fail to navigate them correctly.

Reckoning. The party has done something with
consequences, and someone has come to discuss those
consequences. A powerful being they harmed, who was
more significant than they first realized. A promise they
made and could not keep. An item they lost, or
exchanged, to appease another conflict. At this tier of
play, the actions of the characters have immense
weight; reckoning encounters raise the stakes and give
conflict a more personal feel. They might be
uncomfortable, but they are not meant to punish your
players–use them to deepen the world and its stakes.
Allow the party to respond to what they have done with
the same seriousness the NPC brings to the table. A
perceived betrayal, the consequence of a difficult choice,
a changing alliance–these actions from the party should
not feel like mistakes, but honest decisions, reflecting
on their character and that of the NPC that was
wronged. These encounters can escalate and become
combat events, often making for reluctant battles in
which neither party truly wishes the destruction of the
other. Oh, the drama!
 

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 146

Combat

The third and final axis of any D&D game is Combat. At
higher levels, combat is notoriously inconvenient to run,
with many moving parts, too many dice to roll, and real
difficulty challenging players that are simply leagues
above any stat block you can throw at them. Using the
Legendary Point system described in the introduction
and the powerful features available to 20th+ characters,
confrontations are much easier on the player side. Still,
this is only one part of the equation; as the DM, you
must also do your part to ensure combat at these tiers is
not only enjoyable, but also challenging.

This section focuses on Combat against nonLegendary Foes–the conflicts in between the worldshattering battles of the next section, and how to make
them interesting, even when the stakes are nominally
smaller.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

 
Of Combat in Legendary Tiers

Not every fight in a Legendary Campaign is against a
world-ending threat. Between climactic confrontations,
the party moves through the cosmos, encountering
guards, cultists, lesser monsters, and the armed
representatives of factions that would rather they not
continue. These encounters serve a worldbuilding
purpose – they make the universe feel alive and
continuous. Just because the party has become
Legendary does not mean every goon they meet has
kept pace. These confrontations, however, require some
thought–a Legendary Character dispatching a room full
of ordinary enemies is not a challenge, so it must
definitely not feel like a chore.
Legendary Players in Combat

Without DM intervention, non-Legendary Combat is
often a choice. With the tools available to a Legendary
party, many confrontations can be resolved before
initiative is ever rolled. An inconvenient ambush
neutralized by a well-timed teleportation. A climactic
standoff ended with a single high-level spell. A pursuing
force stopped cold by a Mythical Technique before they
close the distance. These are the representation

of your party’s power level,
able to end conflict before it
 begins, and tend to be among the moments
players remember most fondly.

 Picture this: cosmic pirates attempt to seize the
party’s ship as they travel through the Astral Sea.
Rather than calling for initiative, present it as a

 dramatic encounter. Before the hostiles can board,
the Legendary Fighter uses their Reality Slash Mythical

Technique, sending a wave of destructive

 energy into the pirate vessel and
 destroying its solar sail. Stranded in
 the middle of the stars, the crew

 surrenders. The party spares
them–they are not worth the time. Now
 imagine a hundred pirate ships
 converging at once; still, you present it as
 a scenario, not an immediate combat. The
Cleric thus uses their 9th-level spell to open a Gate and
moves the entire party, ship and all, directly to their
destination.

Where this becomes a problem is on your side of the
screen. If a combat encounter does not reveal
something, threaten something, or change something, it
may not need to exist in the form you have planned.
Overpreparing for confrontations that a Legendary party
will resolve in a single action is a reliable path to
frustration. Build your non-Legendary encounters
loosely, with a clear narrative purpose and room for the
party to engage with them on their own terms. If the
Legendary Wizard reduces an hour of preparation to
one elegant use of their Meteor Swarm, that is not a
failure of the session. Your players are powerful beyond
imagining–give them the space to show off!

Original PDF page 147

Shortcuts

Combat tends to go on for longer than it is enjoyable,
even before 20th level. It is important for you, as the
DM, to know when to take shortcuts when narrating
combat.

The most common shortcut is the narrative
conclusion. When the outcome of a fight is no longer in
doubt–the last two enemies are standing, the party is at
full strength, and everyone at the table knows exactly
how this ends–describe how your players defeat
the final enemies. The Legendary Barbarian cuts
through the remaining soldiers like they were
never a serious consideration, because they
never truly were.

A related tool is the montage encounter, which does
not even require you to call for an initiative roll. The
party must fight their way through an entire wing of a
fortress to reach the throne room. Rather than running
every room as a discrete combat, describe the journey
as a single escalating sequence–the Rogue vanishing
into shadows ahead of the group, the Wizard collapsing
a corridor behind them, the Fighter holding a doorway
against a tide of soldiers. Ask each player what their
character does in the chaos and weave the answers into
a single vivid passage, perhaps including some skill
checks and attack rolls to determine how well they do.
The party reaches the throne room, having spent little
resources and feeling exactly like the unstoppable force
they are supposed to be.

Making Combat Memorable

What makes the non-boss conflicts of your standard
session interesting? What makes them memorable at
your table? Tension. This factor is what separates an
engaging combat from a dull one.

When combat requires a more robust sense of
tension, build the conflict around pressure, novelty, and
variety, rather than attrition or danger. A group of
weaker enemies becomes interesting the moment they
are protecting something, delaying something, or
actively pursuing an objective that the party needs to
stop. A squad of elite celestial soldiers is not a
meaningful fight against a Legendary party–but that
same squad about to execute a prisoner is a different
proposition entirely. A complex battle in a dull setting
might be forgotten, while a straightforward
confrontation that takes place in a paradoxical
dimension where up and down are irrelevant is much
more memorable.

Objectives. The situation must carry as much tension
as the enemies themselves. A Legendary Barbarian can
cut through a dozen demon soldiers without breaking a
sweat, but if those soldiers are buying time for an evil
ritual to finish, every round counts. The fight becomes a
race, regardless of how powerful the party is. Build
toward that tension by establishing the objective before
initiative is rolled. This is what separates an engaging
battle from a simple exchange of damage.

The Setting. The environment deserves
as much attention as the enemies themselves, and
makes these easily forgotten encounters memorable. A
battle against a bunch of high-level mercenaries is not
very engaging, but that same battle amidst a collapsing
astral fortress becomes a movie sequence. The setting
of the battles transforms ordinary combat into
something that demands creative engagement from the
players. The party that finds a way to use the
environment to their advantage, or that loses something
because they failed to account for it, will remember that
fight because of the wacky situation they found
themselves in.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

pages.name: p. 148 - Variety. Enemy variety matters more than enemy

text.content:
Original PDF page 148

Variety. Enemy variety matters more than enemy
quantity at this tier. A large group of identical enemies
resolved in two rounds teaches the party nothing and
costs everyone time. A smaller, more varied group,
composed of different archetypes, creates decisions
worth making even when the outcome is not seriously in
doubt. A spellcaster disrupting concentration from the
back line, a heavily armored juggernaut absorbing
attention at the front, an assassin targeting whoever
looks most exposed. Give your non-boss encounters a
shape, and they become part of the story! Memorable, in
some way, if only for the variety in enemies.
Narrative

Sometimes, combat against random creatures can have
a narrative component to it. Use these encounters to
build context around your larger threats or to deliver a
particular narrative moment.

The slain cultists are wearing the symbol of a
necromancer that the party is looking for. The
mercenary captain who puts up a real fight before going
down is clearly working for someone who can afford the
best soldiers in the cosmos. The demonic warriors have
a commanding officer who manages to escape and warn
his superior in the infernal hierarchy.

Combat is a very useful tool to deliver narrative
through action–seemingly obsolete fights can be
charged with meaning by simply adding an important
detail to be discovered by your party.

Using Monsters

A Legendary Party is powerful enough that balance
becomes a different kind of problem. You are no longer
asking whether the party can handle a given creature–
they almost certainly can. The question is whether the
creature will last long enough to matter, hit hard
enough to create genuine pressure, and do something
interesting before it goes down. True challenges are
reserved for Legendary Foes, creatures beyond CR 30.

The monsters of standard 5e were not designed for
high-level (not even the really powerful ones!), but with
some modifications, they can still serve a purpose at this
tier. Keep them simple. A non-Legendary monster does
not need a complex toolkit–it needs enough hit points to
survive contact, a clear role in the encounter, and one or
two abilities that demand the party’s attention. The
Balor from the 5e SRD is a great example of an
interesting creature to throw at your party; simple,
dangerous, cool-looking, and with enough hit points to
require a certain LP spender to take down.

Here are the general types of monsters, depending on
their abilities, and some tips for running them at
Legendary levels.

Martial-Type. A classic dragon, with high damage, a
powerful rechargeable action, and a substantial pool of
hit points. The Tarrasque is the archetypal example.
These are the easiest to modify for Legendary play–
increasing their hit points is often sufficient to make
them last longer. Action economy really hurts these

THE LEGENDARY GAME

titans, so consider pairing them with lesser creatures
that force the party to split their attention.

Caster-Type. A Lich, an archmage, a powerful
demigod. These creatures are dangerous in regular play
because a single spell can decide a fight. Beyond 20th
level, however, most classes have meaningful tools for
enduring high-level magic, not to mention Legendary
Resistances. This means you can sling powerful spells
at your party without the fear of accidentally removing a
character from the fight entirely. Focus your Casters on
incapacitation rather than raw damage (especially since
spell damage is a bit lackluster at this tier) and
significantly bolster their hit points–a single 20th-level
Fighter can dispatch a Lich in a round without breaking
stride, and that is before accounting for any Legendary
Character rules.

Hybrid. A Solar, a powerful hexblade, a death knight.
These creatures combine special abilities that shift the
flow of battle with the capacity to hold their own in a
straight fight. These require the least modification and
produce the most interesting encounters. Varied
abilities and meaningful special actions matter far more
than additional spells or higher hit points.

Character-Type. A foe that uses the same framework
as the party–class features, action economy, resource
management. These are the most difficult to balance,
and these battles are most often decided by which side
controls the action economy. To account for this, keep
the enemy group smaller than the party and bolster
each character-type enemy with extra hit points.

Original PDF page 149

Legendary Actions & Resistances

When using monsters from standard 5e or
homebrewing your own creatures, the decision to add
Legendary Actions and Resistances comes down to
what you need the encounter to accomplish.

Legendary Actions are standard on most CR 20+
creatures, but they can bog down a fight quickly if
multiple creatures are using them simultaneously. Two
Ancient Dragons, each triggering Legendary Actions at
the end of every player’s turn, is a significant drag on
the session’s pace. If your goal is a challenging
encounter rather than a complex one, consider
foregoing Legendary Actions entirely and simply
increasing hit points and damage output instead. A
creature that hits hard and survives long will create
more tension than one with a busy action economy.

For creatures you want to feel genuinely threatening,
you can opt to use the LP system. Giving a nonLegendary creature 1 or 2 LP and a small number of
features to spend it on is often enough to elevate it into
a meaningful encounter without the overhead of a full
Legendary Foe stat block. A Pit Fiend that uses a
mythical technique, a Lich that casts a 10th-level spell,
or even a Solar with a certain Multiplier Attack; these
are the kind of additions that make a combat
interesting.

Character-type encounters are the best candidates for
introducing the LP system at a smaller scale. Giving
each individual foe 1 LP puts them on near-equal
footing with the party and tends to produce some of the
most dynamic combat interactions a Legendary Game
can offer. The heart of our Legendary Showdowns is the
LP economy, after all!
Survivability

When using regular monsters against Legendary
Characters, some modifications are almost always
necessary if you want them to survive contact with a
single round of Multiplier Attacks. In most cases,
increasing hit points alone is enough to ensure they get
to act. For creatures you intend as mini-bosses, consider
adding 1 or 2 uses of the Legendary Resistance feature.
Note that these creatures will often use the classic
Legendary Resistance and Legendary Actions
framework rather than the LP system, which means
they cannot recover those resistances during the fight–
and also that those resistances are completely separate
from their damage capabilities.

Beyond hit points and Legendary Resistances,
condition immunities, damage immunities, and Limited
Magic Immunity are all tools available to you to make a
creature survive for longer.
Recommended Hit Points

The following table is our own framework for bringing
standard 5e and 5.5e creatures and homebrew
creations up to speed with Legendary Characters. A few
notes before using it.

+

 
This table refers to effective hit points, representing
the total amount of hit points the party must work
through before the creature is defeated. A creature
with 200 hit points is equivalent to a creature with
100 hit points that is resistant to all damage. A
creature that must be fully defeated twice effectively
has double its listed hit points.

+

Character-type foes are not included in this table.
Their hit points vary too widely depending on class
and abilities. As a general guideline, give them 100
to 150 additional hit points above their party
counterpart for each Legendary Tier of Play.

Hit Points vs Legendary Characters

Purpose

Every non-boss encounter should earn its place at the
table. Legendary Characters are not a party to be worn
down by many small battles–their most powerful
abilities (LP spenders) refresh between fights, and
many of them have reliable ways to recover hit points
given a minute. Resource drag is not a meaningful
design philosophy at this tier, and encounters built
around it will feel like speed bumps rather than gritty
survival.

Before placing any non-boss combat in your session,
ask yourself once more: what does this encounter add?
Does it reveal something about the world or the threat
ahead? Does it put pressure on an objective the party
cares about? Does it give a particular character a
moment to shine, or force the group into an interesting
decision? If the answer to all of those is no, the
encounter probably belongs on the cutting room floor,
or as a montage or skill challenge. If the army of pit
fiends is not useful to the story… they can stay in hell.
 

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 150

To Battle Legends

The final section of this chapter delves particularly into
Boss encounters with Legendary Foes. What makes a
creature Legendary? What makes it greater than any
other stat block you’ve come across? Well, first of all,
the use of our LP system. These creatures have the
same tools as the players: an ever-recharging pool of
incredibly powerful abilities that put other monsters to
shame.

This section will teach you how to create an
appropriate challenge for your Legendary Party–not
only as the final boss of a campaign, but also to
represent the powerful creatures of your universe.
Grand climactic moments should always be
accompanied by a Legendary Foe, be it an ally or an
enemy, be it the BBEG or a simple mini-Boss fought on
the way to the final confrontation.

Planning a Legendary Battle

What makes a high-level fight feel good? What
ingredients make it feel like the ultimate showdown,
and not a mechanical slog nobody wants to play
through? These are difficult questions, but we’d like to
venture some answers.

An important disclaimer to make is that battling
Legendary Foes is not exactly something fast! Even
when run like clockwork, combat with Legendary
Characters is still a multiple-hour, brain-draining
activity, on account of the many options available to
your party (and also to your bad guys).

But, when done correctly, these incredible
confrontations are worth every second and synapse
expended. Without further ado, let’s start with a general
overview of the things that make up a legendary battle,
and what to look out for as a DM when orchestrating
these grand showdowns.
Narrative Weight

Many D&D monsters are just that: monsters. Even the
Tarrasque, with as much “world-ending” lore as it
currently has, is just a big bag of hit points. It bites, it
swallows, and it kinda sucks.

A Legendary Foe is, first and foremost, a character.
Yes, you can make a big monster be your campaign’s
final boss, but what differentiates a cool encounter with
a titan from the singular most important fight your
players will face is the narrative weight behind it. Most
of the foes presented in this book are deep and
complicated beings, with their own wants and
motivations, as well as their own extensive stories.
These are world-ending villains, elder demigods, and
legendary heroes from the Universe of Strings, who’ve
changed the cosmos with their presence.

Of course, you might not be playing in our expanded
universe, nor be using our lore. The stat blocks present
in the next chapter are, in the end, a mold with which to
do as you please. By making a few tweaks and
adjustments, you can very easily transform Scháthach,
Wielder of Doom, into your own God of Death, or Angel

THE LEGENDARY GAME

of Vengeance, or whatever you need for your particular
world. If the shoe fits…
Action Economy

Same old issue, valid for fighting rats in the city sewers
and demigods in the middle of space. It follows reason
that a legendary foe should act a lot. This means extra
actions, but also unique reactions and bonus actions,
aura-style traits, and regional effects, such as lair
actions. If you don’t keep up the tension, your BBEG
will be overwhelmed before you can blink.

An often-suggested solution is giving the BBEG a host
of minions, but we caution against that. Following the
design philosophy outlined in these pages, a Legendary
Foe will have the tools to put up a deadly fight all on
their own.

For the most part, we believe the BBEG should be the
narrative center of attention and, mechanically, the
main target for the players’ attacks and abilities. It’s a
“lone dragon against an army” sort of approach.

Original PDF page 151

Prep & Improv

There’s no getting around it. Legendary foes have two
or even three pages worth of traits, actions, extra
actions (and sometimes lair actions).

Running a legendary foe will require some heavy
legwork from you as the DM in terms of prep. In
addition to having a good tactical grasp of each of the
monsters’ abilities, you may need to tweak certain
things to fit the needs of your group. Some abilities
might not be doing a whole lot, because your players are
strong against a certain type of save or damage, and
some might be doing too much because none of the
defensive abilities available to the party apply.

Being ready for all eventualities is an impossibility at
this tier of play; changing certain things on the fly–such
as deciding a certain action can only be used a limited
number of times a day or requires recharge–is also
important. No plan survives contact with the enemy, as
the saying goes.

Stay flexible. The most important thing is to make
sure the fight is fun, not impossible. If that means never
using a Legendary Foe’s ultimate ability, then that is the
path you must take. Most of the stat blocks you will
unveil in this book have more abilities than the foe
actually needs; the point is to know what to use, when to
do so, and how to make it memorable. Each table is
different–an artifact may change the course of the battle
entirely, a bad roll at a bad time could take out a
character from the fight, an unforeseen interaction can
make a certain ability redundant; think on your feet and
keep the battle interesting!
Stakes, Conditions & Outcomes

When preparing to run a Legendary Encounter, it is
very important to determine the stakes of the battle.
What constitutes failure for the players? What are the
win conditions for the villains? Perhaps, it is not just a
matter of who punches harder, but of who achieves
their goal first. Indeed, setting proper stakes is
sometimes more important than running the fight itself,
and can allow for many different outcomes: a pyrrhic
victory, a courageous last stand, a loss for villains and
heroes alike, a noble defeat.

The same rules apply for any high-level battle: set the
proper stakes and make the setting interesting. We
recommend adding secondary objectives for the players,
other than defeating the villain. Disrupting a ritual,
destroying an artifact, or completing a set of tasks.
These are the kinds of goals that make or break a
Legendary Encounter.

Picture this: the evil lich is performing a ritual to make
the universe a worse place (for the eleventh time this
century), and the party must stop them! They defeat the
lich, but the ritual is already finished; even though the
villain is vanquished, their actions will forever haunt the
planes. The ritual being completed is the stakes, and
though the players defeated the antagonist, they failed
in their ultimate goal.

Another example: a powerful warlord ascends to
godhood through an unspeakable artifact and intends to
bring an age of terror upon the world. Although the
players do everything in their power to slay him, it is not
enough, and one by one, they fall. The last player
standing, however, manages to destroy the artifact that
gave the warlord his power, making him regress to his
not-so-almighty mortal form. In a rage, the villain kills
the hero, yet is incapable of achieving his goal of total
world domination, and is eventually defeated by another
party.

These two scenarios are examples of how the
equation of victory or defeat can be modified depending
on the outcome of several factors within the Legendary
Encounter. A varied set of stakes, conditions, and
logical developments is key to making these mythical
battles feel like they matter.
Defeating a Legendary Foe

Before running a Legendary Battle, it is quite important
to determine the conditions under which your villain is
totally defeated. Slaying a Legendary Foe takes much
more than just might, and hit points are often not the
only factor to consider. In this book, we outline several
potential ways to conclusively deal with the Legendary
Foes we present. In adapting these stat sheets to your
world, you will probably have to change the conditions
under which the villains are defeated to better fit your
narrative.

Much like an Artifact, these monsters can sometimes
only be dealt with for good in a certain way, with a
certain weapon, or by casting a certain spell. Think of
the big bads of your favorite fantasy series; a set of
conditions must be met for these incredibly powerful
beings to be laid low, and they should be clear to the
players before the fight. Is reducing their Hit Points to 0
enough, or does the Lich possess a phylactery that you
first need to destroy? What kind of binding ritual needs
to be performed to seal a God? Must an artifact be used
to deal the final blow, lest the evil returns to haunt the
land in a hundred years?
