Prepare to Fight!
The Environment

A Legendary Encounter takes an entire session of play
(sometimes several!). It is not something to do after two
or three hours of puzzle solving and dungeon-crawling.
It is imperative for you, as the DM, to finish the previous
session right at the gates of the final encounter, perhaps
even as the villain finishes their monologue and
Initiative is about to be rolled.

This is not only done for dramatic effect (who doesn’t
love a good cliffhanger?), but also for your and your
players’ convenience. Preparing for such a heavily
mechanical fight takes time, and not only on the DM’s
side! You will be amazed at the wacky strategies your
players will come up with, given a week of planning.
Information

D&D is not a video game. You cannot reload a previous
save and try again. The first time you fight a creature is
often the last. For this reason, information is the most
valuable commodity among your players. At this level of
play, it is less about the consistency of damage, round
after round, and more about the big moments that occur
every couple of turns.

Your players do not have the stat sheet of your
Legendary Foe at hand, and have to slowly piece
together the antagonist’s toolkit. Exercise caution and
be very transparent about certain key pieces of
information when revealing the BBEG’s abilities. You
can add some meta-comments, such as “this ability can
do this” or “this attack will deal X amount of damage”
before the villain acts, so that your players can react to
it. Try to avoid big “got you!” moments, such as using a
certain ability that gives immunity to fire damage right
before the wizard drops their meteor swarm. A
Legendary Foe doesn’t need to trap the players to win–
nor should you try to make these monsters win at all
costs! You are telling a story, after all–the stat blocks are
only there to guide you, and often give you more tools
than necessary to create a compelling battle.

THE LEGENDARY GAME

It is quite vital to construct an environment worthy of
your Legendary Foe. We recommend using a battle map
for these Legendary Encounters! Are you fighting in the
BBEG’s lair, or are you drifting through the astral plane,
among the ruins of a cosmic city? Are you battling over
the forlorn remains of a divine temple, or are you
traveling through dimensions while you face the
campaign’s final threat?

As explained before, you should have some time
between your players arriving at the location of the final
battle and you having to actually run it. In this period,
try to determine what makes your environment fun to
play in!

Some of our Legendary Foes have battlemaps that
depict their lairs. Use them as inspiration, should you be
interested in creating your own environments!
Environmental Effects

What makes for a dynamic environment? Interactivity! A
variety of effects determined by the location of the
players and NPCs, along with a healthy dose of magical
objects and features scattered throughout the arena.
Half-finished spells written in the cobblestones of a
forgotten library, ready to be used by a capable
spellcaster. A terrible artifact in the corner of the room
that spews darkness, as unknowable as it is powerful. A
pond of pure divine water in the middle of the arena,
able to rejuvenate whoever drinks from it.

You can also have more overarching, turn-based
effects, such as a starfall that occurs every few rounds,
or a set of portals that connect different locations in the
battle map. Think about it this way: you are about to
spend 6+ hours on a single battle map. It better be fun
to move through!
Changing Arenas

A staple of many high-level battles are effects that
destroy or otherwise change the environment. A meteor
swarm breaks apart the tower where the fight is taking
place, an epic-level spell changes the landscape
completely, or an artifact teleports the battle and all
involved to a new location entirely. We recommend you
add at least one important change to your arena during
the fight, accompanying a phase change from the
Legendary Foe or an important story event.

Size Matters. Make the arena big. You don’t need to
manage a grid for the entire space you are using–a good
ol’ measuring device can do the trick just fine. This
doesn’t mean the battle will take place over 500 square
feet of terrain, but it does give a sense of scale and
allows the higher movement speed of your Legendary
Foes and characters to shine.

The fight will scale down as the phases pass, until by
the end, you will probably be fighting over a much
smaller arena than the one you started in. That is the
intended progression of these encounters. From the big
and imposing battlefield to the deadly eye of the storm!

Original PDF page 153

Phases

Running one of these battles is akin to directing a
movie. You have to make sure the rising tension is well
managed throughout several hours of play! Think of it
as one big set piece, made to maintain your players’
interest across the session. There are three distinct
phases to Legendary Battles.

Discovery

This phase deals with the players learning about the
bad guy and trying things out. Discovering what the
BBEG is capable of is one of the most important factors
in any final battle, and as a DM, you should be very clear
about the dozen-or-so abilities your Legendary Foe
makes use of. Make sure to showcase your villain’s
actions in a way that doesn’t permanently cripple the
party, while still making them seem intimidating and
powerful.

The discovery phase is also where the players get to
know the environment, its dynamic characteristics, and
the many interactable objects around the arena. It’s a
setup stage for you as a DM, in which key NPCs send
messages to the party telling them they will arrive
shortly, villains prepare powerful traps or channel
rituals, and players start coming up with plans to tackle
the situation at hand. Some action is expected of this
phase, with spells and attacks being thrown around. If
you are going to use minions, we recommend having
them be the preamble to the battle, and not the main
focus. This stage is perfect for your party to have
something to do before actually facing their Legendary
Foe.

Take your Time. D&D tends to frontload the tension of
epic battles. The party is at its most vulnerable right at
the beginning! Dragons use their Breath Weapon, highlevel casters throw 9th-level spells with their first
actions, and minions tend to overwhelm when they are
most numerous.

In a Legendary Encounter, make sure to take your
time. Let your players discover the environment, use
their abilities, and learn about the villain. The
Legendary Foes in this book don’t have to jump to the
offensive immediately for the party to be challenged.

During the discovery phase, your antagonist should
not be using their entire toolkit. Showcase their
abilities, reveal some Legendary Point spenders, but
don’t obsess over using every possible Extra Action.
Showcase, but don’t go all out. This will also give you
some leeway if the fight is going extremely poorly and
the party needs a break.
Main Combat

Exactly what it sounds like. This is the longest phase,
and begins when the BBEG does something that
fundamentally changes the flow of the battle. This can
be the inciting incident of the fight or a more dynamic
effect further into the session. A change of Arena, a
particular objective being completed, or an unexpected

event that takes the party by storm. We always
recommend running these battles in a single, long
session, but if you have to split it into two, this is a good
moment to pause, right before the Main Combat proper.
In fact, your players will be very glad to have some time
to strategize after you change the premise of the battle!

During this stage, you should make sure to expend
your players’ features evenly, so that everyone gets their
turn to shine. Small victories, mounting defeats, NPC
cameos, environmental events, and many other such
things should occur every few rounds. Make sure to
maintain your table’s attention, and err on the side of
smoothness whenever combat bogs down. Use average
damage values for your villain, keep player turns fast,
and don’t forget to roleplay!
Climax & Conclusion

Finally, the climax of your battle. This phase occurs
when most abilities are spent, the darkest hour has
occurred (or is currently happening!), and the arena has
shrunken down. The final confrontation! Turns fly by
much quicker, there is not much movement, and the
villain has become desperate, using everything in their
power to either finish their secondary objective or
destroy the party once and for all. This is the time for
heroic sacrifices, cinematic moments, and key rolls of
the dice!

At this time of the 4-6 hour session, you and your
table might feel a little tired. When the conclusion is
clear for everyone to see, it isn’t strictly vital for you to
run the game until the Legendary Foe is reduced to
their last available hit point; be on the lookout for grand
actions from your players in the final rounds of combat
and wrap the whole thing up with a nice, epic,
descriptive cinematic.

We recommend you end the session as the Legendary
Foe (or the party) is defeated. You can deal with the
aftermath and its consequences next time.
 

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Original PDF page 154

Rising Tension

Use your Legendary Foe in such a way as to direct the
tension through this general structure. If your players
are doing badly, don’t twist the knife; give them some
space! And if they are doing a little too well, use the
BBEG’s more powerful abilities. Of course, this is D&D,
we cannot perfectly predict what is going to happen
throughout the battle. Still, as the DM, you have control
not only over the extremely complicated stat sheets of
these Legendary Foes, but also of the battlefield
dynamics and random events that can occur.

In this vein, it is quite important to add some NPCs to
the battle against the BBEG, to patch things up when
your players need them the most, and to better control
the orchestra that is a 4+ hour combat encounter.
Perhaps, for some reason, a fighter has been left 200
feet away from the action, without anyone to help; don’t
let them waste 3 rounds of combat getting to the BBEG!
Have a wizard NPC teleport them closer. You want
spectacle, clash, ups & downs! Directing your battle in
such a way as to benefit the rising tension is the best
way to ensure everyone is kept engaged.

Here are some story beats from a classic 3 act structure
that you can keep in mind while conducting your final
battle.

Anticipation. Initiative has been rolled! Your players
are in the middle of the discovery phase of the battle.
They have no idea what the BBEG is capable of! Let this
tension simmer, do not throw a 9th-level spell on turn
one, do not use the Legendary Foe’s greatest technique.
Let the players have a turn. Allow them to set up, to
prepare around the villain, and have the BBEG
monologue, or cast a few preparation spells, or tend to a
specific ritual they are conducting. Then…

Inciting Incident. An inciting incident is the thing that
kicks off the plot. Within a D&D battle, we like to think
of the Inciting Incident as the thing that first throws off
the players. Perhaps concentration is broken on key
spells, the martial characters are displaced across the
arena, or an important NPC is banished to another
realm.

Mounting Obstacles. You know the type. Things
slowly become more dire as time goes on. A ritual is
about to be completed. The BBEG enters a new, more
powerful form. A character is incapacitated or outright
killed! Be sure to make these obstacles meaningful, but
not damning. Death spirals are, unfortunately, pretty
common in D&D.

Small Victories. For each overall loss, a small to
medium victory for the players should be in order. Often
enough, the dice take care of these for you, but if your
party is having a bad time, be sure to throw them a
bone!

THE LEGENDARY GAME

Darkest Hour & Roaring Triumph. Always darkest
before the dawn! This moment is the hardest to pull off
correctly, because D&D is, after all, a game. If the party
is winning, they will continue winning. If they are
losing… odds are things will get more difficult. We
recommend planning a narrative darkest hour: a phase
transition, a powerful ability, a change in the
environment. Make the final moments of the fight feel
like a desperate struggle, not a forgone conclusion.

And finally, the Roaring Triumph; a critical hit, a
heroic act, a powerful spell–these moments often come
from your players as they overcome the final obstacle
you put in their way. The key to creating a worthwhile
victory is to reverse the villain’s final act!

This is D&D, not a movie!
You don’t need to have all of these moments in mind while
running your final battle. Most of the time, tension takes
care of itself, especially in such high-stakes scenarios. The
dice have a way of telling a compelling story, no matter the
result. Keep these story beats in mind for when tension is
getting stale; don’t try to enforce them at all costs. As with
everything in D&D, go with the flow!

Original PDF page 155

Party Size

The Legendary Foes in this book are balanced for a
party of 4 to 5 Legendary Characters. If your table runs
smaller or larger than that, some adjustments are
necessary.

The simplest approach is to treat hit points as the
primary balancing dial. For every character below 4,
reduce the foe’s hit points by about 15%. A party of 3
will have a bit more trouble with LP, since the Foe will
recover their resources one turn quicker.

If your party is a 2-person group or even a single
player scenario, we recommend adding an NPC–or
several–to balance these encounters. Reducing hit
points is not enough to offset the Legendary Foe’s
capabilities, and will do nothing for the speed at which it
recovers LP. Consider having the enemy regain their LP
every 2 turns, instead of at the start of each of their
turns.

For larger parties of six or more, resist the temptation
to add a second Legendary Foe unless you are
experienced running the LP system. Instead, give the
Legendary Foe additional Legendary Points, allowing it
to act more frequently across the round. For every
character beyond the 5th, add 1 Legendary Point to the
foe’s arsenal and increase hit points by 15%.

You can also play around with the Legendary Tiers. A
party of 6 Legends might have a genuine shot at
defeating an Ascended-tier foe–although with some
difficulty. And depending on the skill of your players,
you may need to make adjustments to your game
regardless; 3 power gamers, min-maxed to the teeth,
can conceivably defeat a boss way out of their bracket,
while 6 or more casual first-timers who have never
played beyond 5th level might struggle against a
creature below their appropriate Legendary tier.

Behind the Screen

Over the years, we’ve learned a thing or two about
running Legendary Encounters. Before presenting you
with the Legendary Foes of this sourcebook, we’d like to
extend our miscellaneous wisdom to you all, in the hope
it will help you run your epic-level battles a little
smoother.
Managing your Players

You have much to do and much to think about during a
battle of such magnitude; you cannot micromanage
everything your table is doing. Your players must help
you out a little by making sure they know their
characters well. Communicate this clearly before the
session, and make sure they keep each other
accountable when, for example, a concentration check
needs to be rolled.

Trust that they will help you out, and above all, make
sure things run smoothly. Do not get bogged down in
discussions over rules or wacky actions your players
want to take. Be direct, say no if you have to, and keep
going. Depending on the size of your table, you might
need to impose a time limit on turns! In such long fights,

your players will have a lot of turns to act, so having one
or two lackluster rounds won’t ruin their fun.
NPCs

NPCs are great. They help smooth out kinks as they
appear, they make for great ways of showcasing your
BBEG’s abilities, and they can aid the party by doing the
things your players might find boring–activating an
artifact, teleporting your party around the battlefield,
finishing off minions…

NPCs can be used to inform of the villain’s attacks
and features, deliver information about the battle, and
enhance the player experience by buffing or healing the
party.

Make sure NPCs don’t take center stage (unless
during a dramatic sacrifice or similar story beat) and,
above all, make sure their turns are lightning fast. Know
how they are going to act beforehand, make sure they
roll their dice quickly, and arbitrarily determine the
outcomes of some actions, if you have to. Your players
won’t typically pay too much attention to the turn-byturn minutiae of NPC management–they are waiting to
play their own character!
Narrative Moments

Narrative should always take precedence over rules,
especially during fights of such epic magnitudes. It so
happens that, during the heavy mechanical endeavor
that is a Legendary Encounter, your players (and
perhaps even you!) will sometimes forget the story in
favor of the mechanics. Turns will go by in which
characters won’t speak to one another–it’s an inevitable
part of these great set pieces. Whenever you can, try to
foster moments of roleplay in between all the action.
Small one-liners, narrative events, quips from NPCs–
whatever you can think of.

We recommend having big, cinematic moments that
break the action whenever the villain or the players do
something of great importance. These changes can even
coincide with the stages of battle previously discussed.
You can also take such occasions for a short snack &
bathroom break.

To further enhance the weight of such moments, you
can have them end combat for a little while, allowing for
some out-of-combat actions that would take a minute or
two to perform, before resetting the Initiative order
when the party and the BBEG are once again face-toface.
